question: What was reported on 26 July 1943, the day after Mussolini's arrest, in Il Messaggero? Read the surrounding days if helpful, then compile your answer.
final_answer:
Based on the comprehensive search of the July 27, 1943 edition of Il Messaggero, I now have enough information to compile my answer. The July 26 issue is absent from the archive, so the first reporting after Mussolini's arrest appears in the July 27 edition. Let me compile my findings.

## What Il Messaggero Reported on July 26, 1943

The issue of Il Messaggero dated July 26, 1943, is absent from the archive, meaning no newspaper was published on that critical day immediately following Mussolini's arrest on July 25. However, the July 27, 1943 edition provides extensive coverage of the dramatic events that unfolded on July 25-26, offering a window into how the fascist regime's official newspaper presented this historic regime change to the Italian public.

The July 27 edition's front page was dominated by news of the formation of Marshal Pietro Badoglio's new government. The main headline "La composizione del Governo Badoglio" (article 1943-07-27_a000) announced "Un'altra giornata di entusiastiche dimostrazioni di popolo acclamante all'Italia, alle Forze Armate, al Re e al Maresciallo" (Another day of enthusiastic demonstrations by the people acclaiming Italy, the Armed Forces, the King and the Marshal).

The newspaper published detailed accounts of the Gran Consiglio del Fascismo meeting of July 24 (article 1943-07-27_a006), which had not convened since December 7, 1939. The article reported that Grandi's motion of no confidence had passed, listing all attendees including Mussolini, Ciano, Grandi, and other fascist leaders. Remarkably, the newspaper openly acknowledged this vote against Mussolini's leadership.

Coverage emphasized the King's assumption of supreme command and Marshal Badoglio's appointment as head of government. The newspaper published Badoglio's appeal to Romans dated July 26 (article 1943-07-27_a048), warning that "demonstrations will not be tolerated" and calling for discipline and work. The proclamation stressed that "the grave hour demands seriousness, discipline, patriotism devoted to the supreme interests of the Nation."

The July 27 edition described spontaneous popular celebrations that erupted on the evening of July 25 following the radio announcement at 11 PM (article 1943-07-27_a066). Romans poured into the streets shouting "Viva l'Italia," "Viva il Re," and "Viva Badoglio," singing the Inno di Mameli and La Canzone del Piave—patriotic songs of the Risorgimento that had been overshadowed during the fascist period. The article described crowds gathering at the Quirinal Palace, tricolor flags appearing on buildings, and emotional scenes of citizens embracing in the streets.

The newspaper's editorial tone emphasized continuity in one crucial respect: the war would continue. German reactions were reported (article 1943-07-27_a033), noting that Berlin attributed the change to Mussolini's poor health and emphasized Italian proclamations about continuing the war and maintaining alliance fidelity.

The complete list of new ministers was published (article 1943-07-27_a018), describing the government as military in character. An editorial (article 1943-07-27_a040) praised the appointments, particularly highlighting the return of Carmine Senise as Chief of Police, calling it a "reintegration" that "aroused unanimous applause."

Notably absent from this coverage was any mention of Mussolini's arrest or whereabouts—he simply disappeared from the narrative, replaced by celebrations of the monarchy and military leadership. The newspaper thus managed the delicate task of reporting a regime change while maintaining the fiction of orderly transition and continued commitment to the Axis war effort.
